sn21.2:1.2: There Ven. Sāriputta addressed the monks:
sn21.2:2.1: “Friends, just now as I was withdrawn in seclusion, this train of thought arose to my awareness:
sn21.2:2.2: ‘Is there anything in the world with whose change or alteration there would arise within me sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair?’
sn21.2:2.3: Then the thought occurred to me:
sn21.2:2.4: ‘There is nothing in the world with whose change or alteration there would arise within me sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair.’”
sn21.2:3.1: When this was said, Ven. Ānanda said to Ven. Sāriputta,
sn21.2:3.2: “Sāriputta my friend, even if there were change & alteration in the Teacher would there arise within you no sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, or despair?”
sn21.2:3.3: “Even if there were change & alteration in the Teacher, my friend, there would arise within me no sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, or despair. Still, I would have this thought:
sn21.2:3.4: ‘What a great being, of great might, of great prowess, has disappeared!
sn21.2:3.5: For if the Blessed One were to remain for a long time, that would be for the benefit of many people, for the happiness of many people, out of sympathy for the world; for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of human & divine beings.’”
sn21.2:3.6: “Surely,” (said Ven. Ānanda,) “it’s because Ven. Sāriputta’s I-making & mine-making and obsession with conceit have long been well uprooted
sn21.2:3.7: that even if there were change & alteration in the Teacher, there would arise within him no sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, or despair.”
